flash_cards.topic_name: The French Revolution and the Idea of the Nation

cards.topic_name: The French Revolution and the Idea of the Nation

cards.text: Peasants, artisans, workers and new businessmen were happy with the new found freedom. The uniform laws, standardized weights and measures and a common national currency were welcomed by the businessmen and small-scale goods producers.
cards.topic_name: The French Revolution and the Idea of the Nation

cards.text: Napoleonic code was introduced in Dutch Republic, Switzerland, Italy and Germany.
cards.topic_name: The French Revolution and the Idea of the Nation
The French Revolution and the Idea of the Nation
The French Revolution and the Idea of the Nation
The French Revolution and the Idea of the Nation

cards.text: The political and constitutional changes that came due to the French Revolution led to the transfer of sovereignty from the monarchy to a body of French citizens.
cards.topic_name: The French Revolution and the Idea of the Nation

cards.text: France was a full-fledged territorial state in 1789 under the rule of an absolute monarch, Louis XVI.
cards.topic_name: The French Revolution and the Idea of the Nation
The French Revolution and the Idea of the Nation
The French Revolution and the Idea of the Nation

cards.text: The Civil Code of 1804 – usually known as the Napoleonic Code did away with all privileges based on birth, established equality before the law, and secured the right to property.
cards.topic_name: The French Revolution and the Idea of the Nation
The French Revolution and the Idea of the Nation

cards.text: Initially, in many places such as Holland and Switzerland, as well as in certain cities like Brussels, Mainz, Milan and Warsaw, the French armies were welcomed as harbingers of liberty. But the initial enthusiasm soon turned to hostility, as it became clear that the new administrative arrangements did not go hand in hand with political freedom
cards.topic_name: The French Revolution and the Idea of the Nation

flash_cards.topic_name: The Making of Nationalism in Europe

cards.text: Till mid-eighteenth century there were no ‘nation-states’ in Europe.
cards.topic_name: The Making of Nationalism in Europe

cards.text: Germany, Italy and Switzerland were divided into kingdoms, duchies and cantons. All were ruled by different rulers.
cards.topic_name: The Making of Nationalism in Europe

cards.text: Eastern and central Europe was under autocratic kingdoms within the territories of which lived diverse people.
cards.topic_name: The Making of Nationalism in Europe

cards.text: People had nothing in common. They did not share any collective identity or a common culture. All of them had different languages and were from different ethnic groups.
cards.topic_name: The Making of Nationalism in Europe
The Making of Nationalism in Europe
The Making of Nationalism in Europe

cards.text: Habsburg Empire included the Alpine regions – the Tyrol, Austria and the Sudetenland – as well as Bohemia, where the aristocracy was predominantly German-speaking. It also included the Italian-speaking provinces of Lombardy and Venetia.
cards.topic_name: The Making of Nationalism in Europe
The Making of Nationalism in Europe
The Making of Nationalism in Europe
The Making of Nationalism in Europe

cards.text: The differences did not easily promote a sense of political unity. The only tie binding these diverse groups together was a common allegiance to the emperor.
cards.topic_name: The Making of Nationalism in Europe
The Making of Nationalism in Europe
The Making of Nationalism in Europe
The Making of Nationalism in Europe

cards.text: In Western and parts of Central Europe the growth of industrial production and trade meant the growth of towns and the emergence of commercial classes whose existence was based on production for the market.
cards.topic_name: The Making of Nationalism in Europe

cards.text: Industrialisation began in England in the second half of the eighteenth century, but in France and parts of the German states it occurred only during the nineteenth century.
cards.topic_name: The Making of Nationalism in Europe
The Making of Nationalism in Europe

cards.text: In Central and Eastern Europe, new social groups were smaller in number till the late nineteenth century.
cards.topic_name: The Making of Nationalism in Europe

cards.text: After aristocratic privileges were abolished, ideas of national unity became more popular among the educated, liberal middle classes.​​​​​​​
cards.topic_name: The Making of Nationalism in Europe

cards.text: Ideas of national unity in early nineteenth century Europe were closely allied to the ideology of liberalism.
cards.topic_name: The Making of Nationalism in Europe
The Making of Nationalism in Europe
The Making of Nationalism in Europe
The Making of Nationalism in Europe
The Making of Nationalism in Europe
The Making of Nationalism in Europe
The Making of Nationalism in Europe
The Making of Nationalism in Europe

cards.text: Following the defeat of Napoleon in 1815, European governments were driven by a spirit of conservatism.
cards.topic_name: The Making of Nationalism in Europe

cards.text: The conservatives believed that established, traditional institutions of state and society should be preserved.
cards.topic_name: The Making of Nationalism in Europe

cards.text: Conservatives believed in preserving the monarchy, the Church, social hierarchies, property and the family.
cards.topic_name: The Making of Nationalism in Europe

cards.text: Most of Conservatives also wanted to retain the modernization which Napoleon carried out in the spheres of administration.
cards.topic_name: The Making of Nationalism in Europe

cards.text: The conservatives believed that modernization would strengthen traditional institutions.
cards.topic_name: The Making of Nationalism in Europe
The Making of Nationalism in Europe

cards.text: In 1815, representatives of the European powers – Britain, Russia, Prussia and Austria who had collectively defeated Napoleon, met at Vienna to draw up a settlement for Europe.
cards.topic_name: The Making of Nationalism in Europe

cards.text: The Vienna Congress was hosted by the Austrian Chancellor Duke Metternich. The delegates drew up the Treaty of Vienna of 1815.
cards.topic_name: The Making of Nationalism in Europe
The Making of Nationalism in Europe
The Making of Nationalism in Europe

cards.text: The autocratic rule after 1815 had caused fear of repression so the liberal-nationalists started to work underground.
cards.topic_name: The Making of Nationalism in Europe

cards.text: The Revolutionaries established many secret societies to train revolutionaries and spread their ideas.
cards.topic_name: The Making of Nationalism in Europe

cards.text: The Revolutionaries opposed monarchical forms of government established after the Vienna Congress.
cards.topic_name: The Making of Nationalism in Europe

cards.text: The Revolutionaries fought for liberty and freedom and thought the creation of a nation-state as a necessary part of their struggle for freedom.
cards.topic_name: The Making of Nationalism in Europe

cards.text: The Revolutionaries formed secret societies to spread ideas and train revolutionaries. For e.g., Giuseppe Mazzini.
cards.topic_name: The Making of Nationalism in Europe
The Making of Nationalism in Europe
The Making of Nationalism in Europe

cards.text: Following Mazzini’s model, secret societies were set up in Germany, France, Switzerland and Poland.
cards.topic_name: The Making of Nationalism in Europe

cards.text: The Conservatives feared Mazzini. Mazzini’s non-stop opposition towards monarchy and his vision of bringing democratic republics was a reason of fear among many conservatives.
cards.topic_name: The Making of Nationalism in Europe
The Making of Nationalism in Europe
The Making of Nationalism in Europe

cards.text: During the period of 1830-48, Europe witnessed great tension and unrest.
cards.topic_name: The Age of Revolutions 1830 - 1848
The Age of Revolutions 1830 - 1848

cards.text: Revolutionaries belonged to the educated middle-class elite; like professors, school teachers, clerks and members of the commercial middle classes.
cards.topic_name: The Age of Revolutions 1830 - 1848
The Age of Revolutions 1830 - 1848
The Age of Revolutions 1830 - 1848
The Age of Revolutions 1830 - 1848
The Age of Revolutions 1830 - 1848
The Age of Revolutions 1830 - 1848

cards.text: Culture played an important role in creating the idea of the nation: art and poetry, stories and music helped express and shape nationalist feelings.
cards.topic_name: The Age of Revolutions 1830 - 1848
The Age of Revolutions 1830 - 1848

cards.text: Romantic artists and poets criticised the glorification of science and reason. To create a sense of a shared collective heritage and common cultural past they tried to focus on emotions, intuitions and mystical feelings.
cards.topic_name: The Age of Revolutions 1830 - 1848

cards.text: Vernacular languages and folklores strengthen the national spirit.
cards.topic_name: The Age of Revolutions 1830 - 1848
The Age of Revolutions 1830 - 1848
The Age of Revolutions 1830 - 1848
The Age of Revolutions 1830 - 1848

cards.text: The 1830s were years of great economic hardship in Europe.
cards.topic_name: The Age of Revolutions 1830 - 1848

cards.text: Enormous increase in population all over Europe.
cards.topic_name: The Age of Revolutions 1830 - 1848

cards.text: In most countries there were more seekers of jobs than employment.
cards.topic_name: The Age of Revolutions 1830 - 1848

cards.text: Population from rural areas migrated to the cities to live in overcrowded slums.
cards.topic_name: The Age of Revolutions 1830 - 1848

cards.text: Small producers in towns faced with stiff competition from imports of cheap machine-made goods from England
cards.topic_name: The Age of Revolutions 1830 - 1848

cards.text: The rise of food prices or a year of bad harvest led to widespread pauperism in town and country.
cards.topic_name: The Age of Revolutions 1830 - 1848
The Age of Revolutions 1830 - 1848
The Age of Revolutions 1830 - 1848
The Age of Revolutions 1830 - 1848

cards.text: Contractors used to provide the weavers with the raw material so that they could turn them into finished products.
cards.topic_name: The Age of Revolutions 1830 - 1848

cards.text: The payments made to the weavers by these contractors were reduced to a very low amount. This reduction in payment was a reason for this revolt.
cards.topic_name: The Age of Revolutions 1830 - 1848

cards.text: The Revolution of the Liberals in 1848 was led by the educated middle classes.
cards.topic_name: The Age of Revolutions 1830 - 1848
The Age of Revolutions 1830 - 1848

cards.text: Men and women of the liberal middle classes combined their demands for constitutionalism with national unification.
cards.topic_name: The Age of Revolutions 1830 - 1848
The Age of Revolutions 1830 - 1848
The Age of Revolutions 1830 - 1848

cards.text: In the German region, a group of political association that consisted of middle-class professionals, businessmen, and rich artisans gathered in the city of Frankfurt and decided to vote for an all-German National Assembly.
cards.topic_name: The Age of Revolutions 1830 - 1848

cards.text: Elected representatives gathered at Church of St. Paul to take their places in the Frankfurt parliament.
cards.topic_name: The Age of Revolutions 1830 - 1848

cards.text: They drafted a constitution for a German nation to be headed by a monarchy subject to a parliament.
cards.topic_name: The Age of Revolutions 1830 - 1848

cards.text: When the deputies offered the crown to Friedrich Wilhelm IV, King of Prussia on these terms, he rejected the offer and joined other monarchs to oppose the elected assembly.
cards.topic_name: The Age of Revolutions 1830 - 1848

cards.text: The opposition of the aristocracy and military to the parliament grew stronger. Meanwhile, the social base of the parliament eroded because it was dominated by the middle classes.
cards.topic_name: The Age of Revolutions 1830 - 1848

cards.text: The middle class resisted and never paid attention to the demands of workers and artisans and thus lost their support. Finally, troops were called, and the assembly was forced to leave.
cards.topic_name: The Age of Revolutions 1830 - 1848

cards.text: The issue of extending political rights to women was a controversial one within the liberal movement, in which large numbers of women had participated actively over the years.
cards.topic_name: The Age of Revolutions 1830 - 1848
The Age of Revolutions 1830 - 1848

cards.text: In spite of that, they were denied the voting rights during the election of the Assembly. At Frankfurt parliament, women were allowed only as observers to stand in the visitors’ gallery.
cards.topic_name: The Age of Revolutions 1830 - 1848

flash_cards.topic_name: The Making of Germany and Italy

cards.topic_name: The Making of Germany and Italy
The Making of Germany and Italy
The Making of Germany and Italy

cards.text: The new state laid emphasis on modernizing the currency, banking, legal and Judicial systems in Germany. Prussian measures and practices later on became a model for the rest of Germany.
cards.topic_name: The Making of Germany and Italy
The Making of Germany and Italy

cards.text: Italians were scattered over several dynastic states as well as the multi-national Habsburg Empire.
cards.topic_name: The Making of Germany and Italy

cards.text: Italy was divided into seven states during the middle of the nineteenth century. Out of seven, only one Sardinia-Piedmont was ruled by an Italian Princely House.
cards.topic_name: The Making of Germany and Italy
The Making of Germany and Italy
The Making of Germany and Italy
The Making of Germany and Italy
The Making of Germany and Italy
The Making of Germany and Italy

cards.text: In Britain the formation of a nation-state was not the result of a sudden upheaval, revolution or national struggle but of a long drawn-out parliamentary process.
cards.topic_name: The Making of Germany and Italy

cards.text: Britain was not a nation-state prior to the 18th century. British Isle had people of many ethnic groups.
cards.topic_name: The Making of Germany and Italy
The Making of Germany and Italy

cards.text: The English nation steadily grew in wealth, importance and power and extended her influence over other nations of the island.
cards.topic_name: The Making of Germany and Italy

cards.text: The English parliament seized power from the monarchy in 1688 after a conflict.
cards.topic_name: The Making of Germany and Italy

cards.text: In 1688, a nation-state was formed with England at its center.
cards.topic_name: The Making of Germany and Italy

cards.text:
Act of Union

  1. 1707
  2. Act of Union came into being between England and Scotland.

Resulted in the formation of the 'United Kingdom of Great Britain'

  1. England imposed its influence on Scotland.
  2. English members dominated in British parliament.

British power suppressed culture and political institutions of Scotland

  • Catholic clans in Scottish Highlands suffered terrible repression.
  • Forbidden to speak their Gaelic language and to wear their national dress.
  • Large numbers were forcibly driven out of their homeland.
cards.topic_name: The Making of Germany and Italy
The Making of Germany and Italy
The Making of Germany and Italy

cards.text: Artists in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries found a way out by personifying a nation. Artists used female figures to personify a nation.
cards.topic_name: Visualising the Nation
Visualising the Nation

cards.text: To represent ideals symbols or specific objects were used by the artists. For example, the symbols used for the Liberty are red cap or the broken chain. For Justice is generally represented by a blindfolded woman carrying a pair or weighing scales.
cards.topic_name: Visualising the Nation

cards.text: Female allegories were invented by artists in the nineteenth century to represent the nation.
cards.topic_name: Visualising the Nation
Visualising the Nation
Visualising the Nation

cards.text: An area known as the Balkans emerged as the most desired area to fulfill the imperialist goals by the Europeans.
cards.topic_name: Nationalism and Imperialism

cards.text: The Balkans comprised of modern-day Romania, Bulgaria, Albania, Greece, Macedonia, Croatia, Bosnia-Herzegovina, Slovenia, Serbia, and Montenegro.
cards.topic_name: Nationalism and Imperialism

cards.text: A large part of the Balkans was under the control of the Ottoman Empire.
cards.topic_name: Nationalism and Imperialism

cards.text: The Balkans was a region of great geographical and ethnic variation and its inhabitants were known as Slavs.
cards.topic_name: Nationalism and Imperialism

cards.text: The Balkan issue is considered to be one of the major reasons for the First World War.
cards.topic_name: Nationalism and Imperialism
Nationalism and Imperialism

cards.text: The regions with European subjects under its control began to separate away from it and formed up as independent states.
cards.topic_name: Nationalism and Imperialism

cards.text: The Balkan people stressed on independence on the basis that they were once independent nationalities taken over by the foreign powers.
cards.topic_name: Nationalism and Imperialism

cards.text: The rebellious nationalities in the Balkans thought of their struggles as attempts to win back their long-lost independence.​​​​​​​
cards.topic_name: Nationalism and Imperialism

cards.text: During this period of time, the situation became more intense because on one side Balkans were entering into rivalries and on the other side Europeans came into the scene.
cards.topic_name: Nationalism and Imperialism

cards.text: The European powers entered into a rivalry with each other so as to establish their trade and colonies in the Balkan region.
cards.topic_name: Nationalism and Imperialism
Nationalism and Imperialism
Nationalism and Imperialism
